evidence_entities.text: A 66-year-old woman was referred to the hospital due to pronounced weakness, loss of appetite, apathy, weight loss, newly diagnosed diabetes mellitus, and poorly controlled hypertension.
The biochemical evaluation suggested ACTH-dependent hypercortisolemia with severe hypokalemia, metabolic alkalosis, and hyperglycemia.
Markedly elevated levels of metanephrines, along with imaging showing a heterogeneous adrenal lesion, provided evidence for pheochromocytoma.
Histopathological examination confirmed a pheochromocytoma; however, both anti-ACTH and anti-CRH stainings were negative, leading to a diagnosis of PCS.
In this case, we hypothesize that abundant and chronic secretion of catecholamines from the tumor led to a hypermetabolic and pro-inflammatory state that, in turn, overactivated the HPA axis.

Cortisol may potentiate the secretion of catecholamines by increasing the expression of thyrosine hydroxylase (TH), a rate-limiting enzyme in the catecholamine synthesis pathway; and phenylethanolamine N-methyltransferase, a key enzyme in noradrenaline-to-adrenaline conversion.

notes: This case report describes a rare presentation of pseudo-Cushing's syndrome caused by pheochromocytoma
The diagnosis was confirmed through negative ACTH and CRH staining despite clinical presentation suggesting ectopic ACTH secretion
The proposed mechanism involves catecholamine-induced hypermetabolic state leading to HPA axis overactivation
